title: Monitoring solid phase peptide synthesis
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"wen7b","text":"Fmoc determination\r","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":18}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"kudd8","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"su7eu","text":"Monitoring the resin loading during peptide synthesis gives useful information about the progress of the assembly. This can easily done by quantifying the amount of Fmoc removed at each deprotection/coupling cycle via spectrophotometry.\r","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"bodta","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"t7lkm","text":"\r","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ei9tk","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"bcya1","text":"Kaiser Test\r","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":11}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"uadvu","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"5rnvf","text":"The Kaiser test1 is a qualitative test for the presence or absence of free primary amino groups, and it can be a useful indication about the completeness of a coupling step. The test is based on the reaction of ninhydrin with primary amines, which gives a characteristic dark blue colour. The test requires minimal amounts of analyte and is completed within a few minutes.\r","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sup","offset":15,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"itmpu","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"khynd","text":"\r","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"nrh3o","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"meqpw","text":"Microcleavage\r","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":13}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ydef2","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"0zvpf","text":"When Fmoc removal data show anomalies, before performing a step that requires the use of particularly expensive materials, or after a critical step, and in general when assembling longer sequences, it may be useful to cleave and analyse a small amount of intermediate product.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
materials_text: {"blocks":[{"key":"l6iep","text":"Reagents","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":8}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"mrqk9","text":"Kaiser Test\r","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":11}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"nlgt5","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"4kkxe","text":"A. 0.5 g ninhydrin in 10 mL EtOH\r","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"0pcb2","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"8axyo","text":"B. 0.4 mL of 0.001 M KCNaq in 20 ml pyridine.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"2sy6q","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"bz2u4","text":"Equipment","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":9}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"xpchr","text":"UV spectrometer, UV cell (10 mm), small flasks of glass vials, measuring pipette.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: Collect neatly the piperidine solution (12 mL/mg) used for deprotection (steps 4-5 of the standard protocol) in a small flask or glass vial.
Dilute 1/20 with 20% piperidine in DMF (in a small vial: 100 μL collected solution + 1.9 mL 20% piperidine). Mix.
Fill the UV cell with 2.7 mL of 20% piperidine in DMF (reference solution), place the cell into the spectrophotometer and zero at λ=301 nm.
Add 300 μL of the solution prepared at step 2 into the cell, mix and measure the absorbance.

Calculate the loading using the following equation: Loading (mmol/g) = Abs sample x 0.4 a abased on ε 301 =6000 M -1 cm -1 (ε depends also on the specifications of the spectrometer!); 3 mL deblocking solution, 1/200 dilution, 250mg resin. Constance (or slight progressive decrease) of loading is an indication of good progress of the synthesis. If loading values vary irregularly or decrease drastically, a microcleavage test (described below) should be performed.
Transfer a few resin beads to a small glass tube and wash several times with ethanol.
Add 100 μL of each of the solutions above.

Mix well and place the tube in a pre-heated oven (115 °C) for 5 min. ▲ CRITICAL STEP To reduce the incidence of false negative results, it is recommendable to carry out a parallel positive control. The test is not applicable to N-terminal proline residues (secondary amine) and N-alkyl amino acids. The test may give false negative results when applied to aggregate sequences.
Transfer a sample containing ~1-2 mg dry peptide-resin to a small syringe (2 mL).
Add 300 μL of the cleavage cocktail [TFA/ H 2 O/ phenol/ TIPS 8.5/ 0.5/ 0.5/ 0.5] to the dried peptide resin, stir gently for 30 sec and wait for 3 h (stir gently in-between).
Collect the solution in a small HPLC vial, dilute with 400 μL ACN/water 1/1, mix.
At this point the solution can be analyzed in an analytical HPLC system (inject 20 μL) and/or further diluted (1/10) to be injected (2 μL) in LC-MS.

Solid-Phase Peptide Synthesis: From Standard Procedures to the Synthesis of by Irene Coin, Michael Beyermann & Michael Bienert published in Nature Protocols on 24 Oct, 2007 https://dx.doi.org/10.1038/nprot.2007.454
